summary: Joel Spolsky examines the fundamental philosophical difference between Apple and Microsoft's approaches to font rendering on screens. Apple prioritizes preserving the typeface designer's intent even if text appears slightly blurry, while Microsoft prioritizes on-screen readability by snapping letters to pixel boundaries even if it distorts the typeface. This difference stems from Apple's desktop publishing heritage versus Microsoft's pragmatic focus on screen reading. Joel argues that user preferences in this debate are largely driven by familiarity rather than objective quality, with each platform's users predictably preferring what they're accustomed to.
key_points: Apple's font rendering preserves typeface design fidelity at the cost of on-screen blurriness, rooted in its desktop publishing legacy
Microsoft's ClearType hammers fonts into pixel boundaries for sharper on-screen reading, even distorting the original typeface design
Microsoft pragmatically designed entire font families like Georgia and Verdana around pixel boundaries, which look great on screen but lack character in print
User preference surveys on font rendering mostly reflect familiarity bias rather than informed aesthetic judgment, with each platform's users preferring their own system
Windows users experiencing Apple's rendering through Safari for Windows perceive it as blurry, but this reaction is driven by unfamiliarity rather than objective inferiority
money_quotes: Apple generally believes that the goal of the algorithm should be to preserve the design of the typeface as much as possible, even at the cost of a little bit of blurriness.
Microsoft generally believes that the shape of each letter should be hammered into pixel boundaries to prevent blur and improve readability, even at the cost of not being true to the typeface.

Microsoft pragmatically decided that the design of the typeface is not so holy, and that sharp on-screen text that's comfortable to read is more important than the typeface designer's idea of how light or dark an entire block of text should feel.

Typically, Apple chose the stylish route, putting art above practicality, because Steve Jobs has taste, while Microsoft chose the comfortable route, the measurably pragmatic way of doing things that completely lacks in panache.
To put it another way, if Apple was Target, Microsoft would be Wal-Mart.
In most matters of taste, when you do preference surveys, you'll find that most people don't really know what to choose, and will opt for the one that seems most familiar.
Whoa! That's different. I don't like different. Why don't I like these fonts? Oh, when I look closer, they look blurry. That must be why.
key_insight: The Apple vs. Microsoft font rendering debate reveals that most user preferences about design are driven by familiarity rather than objective quality, and both approaches represent legitimate but fundamentally different philosophies about whether screens should serve print fidelity or on-screen comfort.
